title: Multisummable functions and o-minimality
speaker: Patrick Speissegger
abstract: To show that the Gamma function, restricted to the positive real half-axis, generates an o-minimal structure over the real field, we had to show (in collaboration with Lou van den Dries) that the expansion of the real field by all functions that are multisummable in the positive real direction is o-minimal. In the more recent context of the Pila-Wilkie estimates, this structure is receiving some renewed interest. I will summarize some of these new developments concerning multisummability and o-minimality.
tags: Workshop on Special Cycles and Related Topics

proof_stages.label: O-minimal definability problem for special-function germs
proof_stages.summary: The talk poses the problem of deciding when real germs and complex continuations of functions are definable in an o-minimal expansion of the real field, with gamma and zeta chosen as guiding test cases.

proof_stages.label: Transseries control of the Hardy field of R_an,exp
proof_stages.summary: The speaker recalls the Hardy field of one-variable germs definable in R_an,exp, constructs the formal field of transseries, and states the van den Dries-Macintyre-Marker embedding theorem together with truncation-closedness, asymptotic-expansion, finitely generated support, and convergence consequences.

proof_stages.label: Model-theoretic proof mechanism for the embedding
proof_stages.summary: Model completeness and universal axiomatization of R_an,exp with logarithm imply that definable functions are piecewise terms, so induction on terms produces convergent transseries and the injective correspondence with definable germs.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic obstructions to definability in R_an,exp
proof_stages.summary: The transseries embedding rules out definability of the zeta germ via its generalized power-series expansion after substituting log x, and rules out gamma via the divergent Stirling/Binet asymptotic expansion.

proof_stages.label: Generalized-power-series enlargement for zeta
proof_stages.summary: To capture zeta, the talk enlarges the restricted analytic structure by germs represented by convergent generalized power series on corners, then outlines the resolution-of-singularities and Gabrielov-style argument proving o-minimality and model completeness after adding exponentiation.

proof_stages.label: Multisummability and multivariable quasi-analytic algebras
proof_stages.summary: For the gamma obstruction, the speaker defines one-summability and k-/multi-summability through overconvergent holomorphic decompositions on sectors, notes quasi-analyticity and algebraic closure properties, and explains the multivariable polysector framework needed for blow-ups and resolution.

proof_stages.label: Multisummable o-minimal structure and gamma definability
proof_stages.summary: The real traces of multisummable germs are added to the language to form a structure whose Taylor map is injective and whose o-minimal/model-complete expansion with exponentiation defines the gamma germ, with directional definability obtained by rotation within suitable sectors.

proof_stages.label: Complex-domain refinements in Q&A
proof_stages.summary: In response to questions, the speaker states domain-level definability results for zeta on right-unbounded but imaginary-bounded strips, nondefinability on imaginary-unbounded sets, analogous gamma-domain results bounded by argument level curves, and related transseries/general Dirichlet-series remarks.

examples.name: Riemann zeta function as a running test germ
examples.note: The zeta function is used to turn the abstract definability question into the concrete obstruction that zeta(log x) has a convergent generalized power-series expansion with non-finitely generated support, motivating the larger generalized-power-series structure.
examples.engagement_reason: He composes zeta with log, writes it as a convergent generalized power series, uses its asymptotic expansion/support to prove non-definability in R_an,exp, and later discusses definability on strips.

passes.reason: the ordinary special function zeta is used to show a germ not definable in R_an,exp via its generalized-power-series support.
The zeta germ is used to refute definability in R_an,exp via its log-pullback generalized power-series support, but it is not structurally degenerate, extremal, symmetric, or exceptional.
The zeta germ is a structurally ordinary Dirichlet-series/special-function example used to show non-definability in R_an,exp.
The Riemann zeta germ is an ordinary special function used to show non-definability in R_an,exp.
Used as a standard special-function germ whose generalized-power-series asymptotics show non-definability in R_an,exp.
used to show the zeta germ is not definable in R_an,exp, though zeta is a standard special function rather than a structural exception

used to show the Riemann zeta germ is not definable in R_an,exp, while zeta itself is a standard special function with no extremal, degenerate, symmetric, or sporadic classification-exception structure here
Riemann zeta is used to show failure of definability in R_an,exp, while it is not structurally sporadic, extremal, degenerate, or maximally symmetric.
used to prove the zeta germ is not definable in R_an,exp, while zeta is a standard Dirichlet/L-function rather than structurally exceptional
Used to show the zeta germ is not definable in R_an,exp via its generalized power-series asymptotics, while zeta itself is not structurally sporadic or extremal.
Used to refute definability in R_an,exp through the non-finitely generated support of zeta after logarithmic substitution.
The zeta germ is used to show definability in R_an,exp fails, via its generalized power-series expansion with non-finitely generated support.

examples.name: Gamma function and the Binet-Stirling correction
examples.note: Gamma is the parallel test case whose Binet/Stirling correction has a divergent but multisummable asymptotic series, thereby motivating the multisummable o-minimal structure.
examples.engagement_reason: He uses Binet’s second formula to isolate the Stirling correction phi, analyzes its divergent asymptotic expansion and one-summability, and applies this to Gamma’s definability.

passes.reason: gamma and its Binet-Stirling correction are standard special-function germs used to refute R_an,exp definability through a divergent asymptotic series.

Gamma, through the Binet-Stirling correction with divergent asymptotic expansion, is used as an obstruction to R_an,exp definability, while the functions themselves are ordinary special functions structurally.
Gamma via the Binet-Stirling correction is an ordinary classical special-function example whose divergent asymptotic expansion obstructs definability in R_an,exp.
Gamma and its Binet-Stirling correction are ordinary special-function germs used via divergent asymptotics to rule out R_an,exp-definability.
The divergent Binet-Stirling correction is used to rule out definability of Gamma in R_an,exp despite later definability in a larger multisummable structure.
the Binet-Stirling correction’s divergent asymptotic expansion is used to rule out Gamma’s definability in R_an,exp
used via the divergent Binet-Stirling correction to show Gamma is not definable in R_an,exp, but the Gamma/Binet correction is not structurally extremal, degenerate, symmetric, or exceptional

Gamma via the Binet-Stirling correction is used as an obstruction to R_an,exp-definability through its divergent asymptotic expansion, but the function is structurally ordinary among special functions.
used via the divergent Binet-Stirling correction to prove Gamma is not definable in R_an,exp, with no intrinsic sporadic or extremal structure involved
Used to show Gamma is not definable in R_an,exp because the Binet-Stirling correction has a divergent asymptotic expansion.
Used to refute definability in R_an,exp because the Binet-Stirling correction has a divergent asymptotic power series.
The Gamma/Binet-Stirling correction example obstructs R_an,exp-definability because the correction has a divergent asymptotic expansion.

examples.name: Generalized series with accumulating exponents
examples.note: The audience-prompted series such as sum x^(1/n) or sum x^(1-1/n) illustrate why the speaker imposes a natural-support condition on generalized power series.
examples.engagement_reason: He tests concrete accumulating-exponent series such as sums of x^(1/n) or x^(1-1/n) against the natural-support condition and explains why they are not allowed.

passes.reason: the accumulating-exponent series is an excluded non-natural generalized power series illustrating failure of the support condition.
Series such as sums with exponents accumulating at a finite point are boundary non-natural cases used to show the support condition fails.
The accumulating exponent support is a limiting/non-natural generalized-series support used to show that such series are excluded from the allowed natural class.
Accumulating exponents form a limiting support pathology cited as an excluded non-natural generalized series.
Series with accumulating exponents are cited as not satisfying the natural-support condition for the allowed generalized power series.
series such as sums with exponents accumulating at a finite point are invoked as excluded cases violating the natural-support condition
used as a nonallowed support example showing the natural-support condition can fail, and finite accumulation of exponents is a limiting boundary behavior for such generalized series
A generalized series with accumulating exponents is used as a non-example of the allowed natural-support class and is degenerate in lacking the local-discreteness structure required there.
used to show why such generalized series are excluded, and the accumulating exponent support is a limiting non-natural degeneration of the permitted supports
Cited as a forbidden case whose exponents accumulate in a compact interval, so the support degenerates from the required locally finite/natural form.
Used as an excluded generalized-series case whose accumulating exponents lack the natural locally finite support required.
Series such as sums with exponents accumulating at a finite point are given as nonallowed cases violating the natural-support condition.

examples.name: Sum of a one-summable and a two-summable function
examples.note: This toy case shows why single k-summability classes are not closed under addition and leads directly to the notion of (1,2)-summability and then general multisummability.
examples.engagement_reason: He traces what happens when adding a one-summable and a two-summable function, combining the defining F_p and G_p data and identifying the result as one-two-summable.

passes.reason: the sum of one- and two-summable functions is a hybrid example used to show neither summability class is closed under such sums.
A sum of a one-summable and a two-summable function is used to show that single-level summability is not closed under such sums, motivating multisummability.
A sum of one- and two-summable functions is structurally a typical mixed multisummable object used to show single-level summability is not closed under such sums.
A generic mixed summability-level sum is used to show that single k-summability classes are not closed under such sums.
A generic sum of one-summable and two-summable functions is used to show that single-level summability is not closed under addition.
the sum is used to show that single-level k-summability is not closed under adding functions of different summability levels
used to show that adding different summability levels need not stay one- or two-summable, though the mixed sum is an ordinary multisummable object rather than structurally special
The sum of a one-summable and a two-summable function is used to show that single-level summability is not closed under such sums, though the mixed multisummable object is structurally typical.
used to show that adding a one-summable and a two-summable function need not remain in either single summability class
Used to show that adding functions of different summability orders need not stay one- or two-summable, motivating multisummability.
Used to show that mixing a one-summable and a two-summable function need not yield either kind separately.
The sum of a one-summable and a two-summable function is used to show that a single k-summability class is not closed under such sums.

examples.name: Convergent Dirichlet series beyond zeta
examples.note: In Q&A, convergent Dirichlet series are mentioned as further functions whose logarithmic change of variables gives convergent generalized power series, illustrating that the zeta argument is not special to zeta alone.
examples.engagement_reason: He only states that any convergent Dirichlet series can similarly be transformed into a convergent generalized power series, without working out a specific series or argument.

passes.reason: convergent Dirichlet series are treated as ordinary positive instances transformable into convergent generalized power series.

Convergent Dirichlet series are presented as a typical family transformed to convergent generalized power series and positively definable in the larger structure.

Convergent Dirichlet series are ordinary extensions of the zeta example, transformed into convergent generalized power series for definability in the larger structure.

used as a positive generalization of zeta to Dirichlet series definable after transformation to convergent generalized power series
